items.q: What is the IPA in linguistics?
items.correct: The International Phonetic Alphabet — a standardised set of symbols where each symbol represents one distinct sound. Used to transcribe pronunciation precisely across all languages.
items.misconception: The IPA is just another alphabet like the Roman or Greek alphabet.
items.random_wrong: The IPA stands for International Phonetic Acronym.
items.why: Ordinary alphabets have ambiguous symbol-sound mapping (English 'ough' has multiple pronunciations). The IPA exists precisely because regular alphabets are insufficient — one symbol = one sound, universally.

items.q: What is an adjacency pair in conversation analysis?
items.correct: A two-part sequence where the first part conditions the second — question/answer, greeting/greeting, request/acceptance-or-refusal. The second part is 'conditionally relevant' on the first.
items.misconception: Adjacency pairs are two people who sit next to each other while talking.
items.random_wrong: Adjacency pairs are pairs of synonyms in vocabulary lists.
items.why: Adjacency pairs reveal that turns aren't independent — a question expects an answer, and silence after one is heard as meaningful. Students take 'adjacency' too literally (physical proximity) rather than structural sequencing.

items.q: What is overextension in child language?
items.correct: When a child applies a word more broadly than adults do — e.g. calling all four-legged animals 'dog' before learning distinct categories.
items.misconception: Overextension is when children use very long words they don't understand.
items.random_wrong: Overextension is when children speak too loudly.
items.why: Overextension is about semantic categories, not word length or volume. It shows children are forming hypotheses about meaning, then refining them — a key window into acquisition.

items.q: A short text under a picture is a.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Caption. “Pull quote” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A headline that lures clicks but underdelivers is.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Clickbait. “Pun” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many media-text features here (headline, subheading, pun, clickbait, caption, pull quote)?
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
